prompt: Chloe is 20 years old and a full-time student. She lives in a dorm but stays with her father, Mark, during breaks. Mark provides 70% of her support. Chloe earned $5,000 from a summer internship and is unmarried. Is Chloe a dependent?
chosen: Yes, Chloe is a Qualifying Child (QC) under § 152(c). As a student under age 24, she meets the age test (§ 152(c)(3)(A)(ii)). Her time at school is a temporary absence, satisfying residency with Mark (§ 152(c)(1)(B)). Since she did not provide over half of her own support and is unmarried, she qualifies.
rejected: No, Chloe is not a dependent because she is over 19 and her $5,000 income exceeds the gross income limit for a qualifying relative.

prompt: Robert supports his mother, Helen, who lives in her own apartment. Helen's total support costs are $20,000. Robert pays $11,000; Helen's social security pays the rest. Helen's gross taxable income is $2,000. Is Helen a dependent?
chosen: Yes, Helen is a Qualifying Relative (QR) under § 152(d). Robert provides over half of her support (§ 152(d)(1)(C)), and her gross income is below the exemption amount (§ 152(d)(1)(B)). As a mother, she does not need to live with him to meet the relationship test (§ 152(d)(2)(C)).
rejected: No, Helen is not a dependent because she does not share a principal place of abode with the taxpayer for more than half of the year.

prompt: Maria is 22 years old and lived with her boyfriend, Jose, for the entire year. She has no income and Jose provided 100% of her support. Maria is a US citizen. Is Maria Jose's dependent?
chosen: Yes, Maria is a Qualifying Relative (QR) under § 152(d)(2)(H). An individual who has the same principal place of abode as the taxpayer and is a member of the household for the entire year satisfies the relationship test, even if they share no familial blood or marriage link.
rejected: No, Maria is not Jose's dependent because they do not share a familial relationship defined in § 152(d)(2)(A)-(G).

prompt: David is the 10-year-old son of Sarah's brother. David lives with Sarah all year. His parents earn $40,000 each but do not claim him. Sarah earns $85,000. Can Sarah claim David?
chosen: Yes, Sarah can claim David as a QC. Under § 152(c)(4)(C), a non-parent can claim a child if no parent claims him and the taxpayer's AGI ($85,000) is higher than the highest AGI of any parent ($40,000).
rejected: No, Sarah cannot claim David because parents always have priority for the dependency claim over non-parents regardless of income.

prompt: Oscar is a 24-year-old full-time student. He lives with his parents and earns $3,000. His parents provide all his support. Can they claim him as a Qualifying Child?
chosen: No, Oscar is not a QC. Under § 152(c)(3)(A)(ii), a student must not have attained age 24. Since Oscar is 24, he fails the age test for QC, though he may still qualify as a QR if other tests are met.
rejected: Yes, Oscar is a QC because the age limit for full-time students is inclusive of age 24 under § 152.

prompt: Kevin is 22 years old and lives with his grandmother, Rose. He is a full-time student with no income. Kevin has a daughter, Lily, whom he claims as a dependent on his own return. Can Rose claim Kevin?
chosen: No, Rose cannot claim Kevin. Under § 152(b)(1), if an individual is a dependent of another taxpayer (Kevin claiming Lily), that individual is treated as having no dependents and cannot be claimed by anyone else.
rejected: Yes, Rose can claim Kevin because he meets the age, residency, and support tests to be Rose's Qualifying Child.

prompt: Gary supports his 30-year-old cousin, Phil. Phil lives in a different state but Gary provides 70% of his support. Phil's gross income is $2,000. Can Gary claim Phil?
chosen: No, Gary cannot claim Phil. A cousin is not listed in § 152(d)(2)(A)-(G). Therefore, a cousin must live with the taxpayer for the entire year under § 152(d)(2)(H) to meet the relationship test.
rejected: Yes, Gary can claim Phil because Gary provides over half of Phil's support and Phil's income is below the exemption threshold.

prompt: Tina is 18 years old and lives with her grandmother and mother. The mother earned $12,000; the grandmother earned $45,000. Both claim Tina. Can the grandmother claim Tina?
chosen: No, the grandmother cannot claim Tina. Under § 152(c)(4)(A)(i), if a parent and a non-parent both claim a child as a QC, the child is treated as the qualifying child of the parent.
rejected: Yes, the grandmother is entitled to the claim because her adjusted gross income is higher than the mother's.

prompt: Ben provides 60% support for his father, Abe. Abe received $15,000 in non-taxable Social Security and $3,000 in taxable interest. Is Abe Ben's dependent?
chosen: Yes, Abe is a QR. For the gross income test (§ 152(d)(1)(B)), only taxable income ($3,000) is counted. For the support test (§ 152(d)(1)(C)), all sources are counted, and Ben still provided over half.
rejected: No, Abe is not a dependent because his total income of $18,000 exceeds the maximum gross income allowed for a qualifying relative.

prompt: Sofia's aunt, Clara, has $40,000 in expenses. Sofia pays $3,900 (9.75%), her brother pays $18,100, and Clara's son pays $18,000. Both sign waivers for Sofia. Can Sofia claim Clara?
chosen: No, Sofia cannot claim Clara. Under § 152(d)(3)(C), a taxpayer in a multiple support agreement must contribute more than 10% of the support. Sofia's 9.75% contribution is insufficient.
rejected: Yes, Sofia can claim Clara because the group as a whole provided over 50% of the support and the other members filed written declarations.

prompt: Liam is 20 years old and a full-time student. He receives a $30,000 scholarship for tuition. His father, David, provides $10,000 for room and board. Liam has no other income. Can David claim Liam as a QC?
chosen: Yes, David can claim Liam. Under § 152(f)(5), scholarships for a child who is a student are not taken into account for the support test (§ 152(c)(1)(D)). Thus, David is treated as providing 100% of Liam's support.
rejected: No, David cannot claim Liam because the $30,000 scholarship means Liam provided more than half of his own support.

prompt: Elena is a 20-year-old student living in Mexico. Her uncle, Victor, is a US citizen living in Texas. Victor provides 100% of Elena's support. Elena is a citizen of Mexico. Is Elena a dependent?
chosen: Yes, Elena is a QR. While non-citizens are generally excluded (§ 152(b)(3)(A)), there is an exception for residents of a country contiguous to the US (Canada or Mexico). As a niece, she meets the relationship test.
rejected: No, Elena is not a dependent because she is not a US citizen and does not reside in the United States.

prompt: Mark and Sarah are divorced. Toby (10) lives with Mark for 7 months and Sarah for 5 months. Sarah's AGI is $150,000; Mark's is $40,000. Both claim Toby. Can Sarah claim Toby?
chosen: No, Sarah cannot claim Toby. Under § 152(c)(4)(B)(i), if parents claim a child and do not file a joint return, the claim goes to the parent with whom the child resided for the longest period of time.
rejected: Yes, Sarah can claim Toby because the tie-breaker rules prioritize the parent with the highest adjusted gross income when both claim the child.

prompt: Julia supports her friend, Beth, who moved in on February 1st and stayed through December 31st. Beth had no income and Julia provided all support. Is Beth Julia's dependent?
chosen: No, Beth is not a QR. Under § 152(d)(2)(H), a non-relative must have the same principal place of abode and be a member of the household for the entire taxable year. Missing January disqualifies her.
rejected: Yes, Beth is a QR because she lived with Julia for more than half the year and Julia provided more than 50% of her support.

prompt: Robert, a US citizen, adopts Mei in a non-contiguous foreign country. Mei lives with Robert in that foreign country all year. Mei is not a US citizen. Can Robert claim Mei?
chosen: Yes, Robert can claim Mei. § 152(b)(3)(B) provides an exception for an adopted child of a US citizen if the child has the same principal place of abode as the taxpayer for the entire year, regardless of the country.
rejected: No, Robert cannot claim Mei because she is not a US citizen and does not reside in a country contiguous to the US.

prompt: John provides $12,000 for his girlfriend, Alice. They live together all year in a state where cohabitation is a misdemeanor under an unenforced law. Alice has no income. Can John claim Alice?
chosen: No, John cannot claim Alice. Under § 152(f)(3), an individual is not a member of the household if the relationship between the individual and the taxpayer violates local law.
rejected: Yes, John can claim Alice because the cohabitation law is unenforced and she otherwise meets all tests for a qualifying relative.

prompt: Sam supports his 17-year-old half-sister, Nina, who lives with him. Nina has no income. Does Nina meet the relationship test for a Qualifying Child?
chosen: Yes, Nina meets the test. Under § 152(f)(4), the term 'sister' includes a sister by the half-blood. Nina is a sibling under § 152(c)(2)(B).
rejected: No, Nina does not meet the QC relationship test because half-siblings only qualify as Qualifying Relatives under § 152(d).

prompt: A 16-year-old, Kevin, was placed with the Miller family by a court order. Kevin lived there for 5 months and the Millers provided all support. Is Kevin their QC?
chosen: No, Kevin fails the residency test. While a foster child is a 'child' under § 152(f)(1)(C), they must still live with the taxpayer for more than half the year (over 6 months) per § 152(c)(1)(B).
rejected: Yes, Kevin is a QC because he is an eligible foster child placed by a court, satisfying the relationship test.

prompt: Victor is 25 years old and permanently disabled. He lives with his mother and earns no income. Does Victor meet the age requirement for a Qualifying Child?
chosen: Yes, Victor meets the age requirement. Under § 152(c)(3)(B), age requirements are waived for an individual who is permanently and totally disabled at any time during the year.
rejected: No, Victor is over 24 and not a student, so he must be evaluated as a Qualifying Relative rather than a Qualifying Child.

prompt: Mark provides $5,000 for his son, Leo. Mark's ex-wife, Susan, receives $10,000 in alimony and uses $6,000 for Leo's food. Does Mark's alimony count as support provided by him for Leo?
chosen: No, it does not. Under § 152(d)(5)(A)(i), alimony payments are not treated as a payment by the payor spouse for the support of any dependent.
rejected: Yes, because the funds originated from Mark, they are credited to him for the purposes of the support test.
